name: Guatemalan Pepian de Pollo
steps: Simmer the chicken thigh in water 20 minutes until cooked through; reserve the broth.
Toast the pumpkin seeds in a dry pan until they pop, then blend with the tomatoes and a splash of broth into a sauce.
Simmer the sauce with the shredded chicken 10 minutes. Serve over the cooked rice.
steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken thigh in the chicken broth plus 1 cup water for 20-25 minutes until cooked through. Remove the chicken, shred it, and reserve the broth.
Toast the pumpkin seeds and sesame seeds in a dry skillet over medium heat, stirring often, until the pumpkin seeds pop and turn golden, about 3-4 minutes. Let cool slightly.
In the same skillet, dry-toast the tomatoes, onion, garlic, and bell pepper for 5 minutes to char them lightly.
Blend the toasted seeds with the charred vegetables and 1 cup of the reserved broth until smooth - this is the pepian base.
Pour the sauce back into the pot, stir in the cumin and black pepper, and simmer 10-15 minutes until it thickens slightly, thinning with more broth if needed.
Return the shredded chicken to the sauce and simmer 5 more minutes.
Serve hot over the cooked white rice.

name: Guatemalan Tamales Colorados
steps: Whisk the masa harina with warm water and the achiote paste into a thick, spreadable dough.
Simmer the chicken with the diced tomatoes 15 minutes until cooked; shred and stir into the dough.
Spread the filled dough onto a piece of parchment or a soaked corn husk, fold into a packet, and steam 45 minutes.
steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken thigh with the bay leaf in the chicken broth for 20 minutes until cooked through. Shred the meat and reserve the broth.

Saute the onion, bell pepper, and garlic in the vegetable oil for 5 minutes until softened, then stir in the diced tomatoes and 1 tbsp of the achiote paste. Simmer 8 minutes into a thick recado sauce.
Toss the shredded chicken with half the recado sauce.
Whisk the masa harina with the remaining reserved broth and remaining achiote paste until you have a thick, smooth, spreadable dough - add warm water a little at a time if it's too stiff.
Lay out a square of parchment paper (or a soaked corn husk/banana leaf if you have one), spread a portion of the masa dough in the center, and top with a spoonful of the chicken filling and sauce.
Fold the parchment over the filling to fully enclose it into a sealed packet, then repeat with the remaining dough and filling.
Steam the packets over simmering water for 45-50 minutes, until the masa is firm and pulls cleanly from the wrapper.

name: Guatemalan Frijoles Volteados
steps: Saute the onion in the oil until soft.
Add the black beans with a splash of their liquid and mash them roughly in the pan.
Keep cooking and stirring until the beans pull together into a thick, smooth paste.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion and garlic in the vegetable oil over medium heat for 4-5 minutes until soft and fragrant.
Add the black beans along with the bay leaf and enough of their cooking liquid to keep things loose, and simmer 5 minutes.
Remove the bay leaf, then mash the beans directly in the pan with a fork or potato masher until mostly smooth.
Keep cooking over medium-low heat, stirring often, for 10-12 minutes until the mash pulls together into a thick paste that holds its shape on a spoon.
Serve warm, crumbling the queso fresco over the top.

name: Belizean Stewed Chicken
steps: Rub the chicken with the achiote paste and let it sit 10 minutes.
Melt the brown sugar in a hot pot until it darkens slightly, then add the chicken and brown on all sides.
Add a splash of water, cover, and simmer 25 minutes until the chicken is tender. Serve over the rice.
steps_detailed: Rub the chicken thighs with the achiote paste, lime juice, garlic, and black pepper. Marinate 15-20 minutes (or longer in the fridge if you have time).
Melt the brown sugar in a heavy pot over medium-high heat, swirling gently, until it deepens to a dark caramel - watch closely so it doesn't burn.
Add the marinated chicken and brown it in the caramelized sugar on all sides, about 5-6 minutes.
Add the onion and bell pepper, cooking 3-4 minutes until they start to soften.
Pour in the chicken broth, scraping up anything stuck to the pot, then cover and simmer 25-30 minutes until the chicken is tender and the sauce has thickened.
Serve over the cooked white rice, spooning the dark stewing sauce over the top.

name: Belizean Rice and Beans
steps: Simmer the beans with the coconut milk and garlic for 5 minutes.
Stir in the rice and enough water to cook it, then cover.
Cook 18-20 minutes until the rice is tender and has soaked up the coconut broth.
steps_detailed: In a pot, combine the kidney beans, coconut milk, garlic, onion, black pepper, oregano, and bay leaf with 1 cup water. Simmer 8-10 minutes to let the flavors come together.
Stir in the rice and enough additional water to just cover everything by about half an inch.
Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low, cover tightly, and cook 18-20 minutes without lifting the lid.
Remove the bay leaf, fluff with a fork, and let sit covered 5 minutes before serving.

name: Belizean Fry Jacks with Refried Beans
steps: Mix the flour and baking powder with a splash of water into a soft dough; rest 10 minutes.
Roll into a round and fry in hot oil until puffed and golden on both sides.
Serve with the warmed, mashed pinto beans alongside.
steps_detailed: Rub the butter into the flour and baking powder with a pinch of salt, then add water a little at a time to form a soft, slightly sticky dough. Cover and rest 10-15 minutes.
Meanwhile, saute the onion until soft, add the pinto beans with a splash of water, and mash roughly, cooking until thick.
Divide the dough into two, roll each piece into a thin round, and cut a small slit in the center of each.
Heat the vegetable oil in a skillet over medium-high heat until shimmering. Fry each round 1-2 minutes per side until puffed and deep golden.
Drain briefly on a paper towel, then serve warm alongside the refried beans, crumbling the queso fresco over the beans.

name: Honduran Baleada
steps: Warm the tortillas and the mashed pinto beans.
Spread the beans down the center of each tortilla.
Top with the crumbled queso fresco and sour cream, then fold in half.
steps_detailed: Warm the pinto beans in a small pot, mashing lightly until spreadable but still a little chunky.
Warm each tortilla in a dry skillet with a light scrape of butter until soft and lightly toasted in spots, about 30 seconds per side.
Spread a generous layer of the mashed beans down the center of each tortilla.
Top with the crumbled queso fresco, a drizzle of sour cream, and the sliced avocado.
Fold each tortilla in half and press gently to close before serving.

name: Honduran Tapado Costeño
steps: Simmer the cassava in the coconut milk with a cup of water for 10 minutes.
Add the plantain and cook another 10 minutes until both are tender.
Add the shrimp and simmer 4-5 minutes until just cooked through.
steps_detailed: Bring the coconut milk, 1.5 cups water, onion, bell pepper, garlic, and achiote paste to a simmer in a wide pot.
Add the cassava and simmer 12-15 minutes until it starts to soften.
Add the plantain and continue simmering 8-10 minutes until both the cassava and plantain are fork-tender.
Add the shrimp and simmer gently 4-5 minutes, just until pink and cooked through - don't let it boil hard or the shrimp will toughen.
Stir in the cilantro just before serving.

name: Honduran Yuca con Chicharron
steps: Boil the cassava chunks 20 minutes until fork-tender; drain.
Fry the pork cubes in their own fat until deeply browned and crisp.
Serve the yuca topped with the crispy pork and shredded cabbage, with the lime juice squeezed over.
steps_detailed: Boil the cassava in salted water 20-25 minutes until completely fork-tender; drain well.
Season the pork cubes with black pepper. Heat the vegetable oil in a heavy skillet over medium heat and add the pork.
Cook the pork slowly, 15-18 minutes, letting its own fat render out, until the pieces are deeply browned and crisp on the outside - stir occasionally.
Toss the shredded cabbage, carrot, and red onion with the lime juice for a quick slaw.
Serve the boiled cassava topped with the crispy pork and slaw piled on top.

name: Salvadoran Pupusas Revueltas
steps: Mix the masa harina with warm water into a soft, workable dough.
Flatten a ball of dough, fill with the beans, cheese, and pulled pork, then seal and pat back into a disc.
Cook on a hot, dry griddle 3-4 minutes per side until spotted golden brown.
steps_detailed: Mix the masa harina with warm water, a little at a time, kneading until you have a smooth, soft dough that doesn't crack at the edges when pressed - let it rest 5 minutes.
Mash the pinto beans until mostly smooth, and mix the queso fresco and pulled pork together in a separate small bowl.
Divide the dough into balls a bit larger than a golf ball. Flatten each into a disc in your palm, making a slight well in the center.
Spoon a little of the beans and the cheese-and-pork mixture into the well, then fold the edges up and over to seal the filling completely inside.
Gently pat and press the filled ball back out into a flat disc about 1/2 inch thick, patching any tears with a bit of extra dough.
Cook on a lightly oiled hot griddle or skillet over medium heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until golden-brown spots appear and the pupusa is cooked through.

name: Salvadoran Curtido
steps: Toss the shredded cabbage and carrot together in a bowl.
Pour the vinegar over and mix well, pressing to soften the cabbage slightly.
Let sit at least 30 minutes before serving, or overnight for a more pickled flavor.
steps_detailed: Combine the shredded cabbage, carrot, and red onion in a large bowl.
Pour boiling water over the vegetables just to wilt them slightly, then drain immediately and let cool.
Toss the cooled vegetables with the vinegar, oregano, and black pepper, pressing down to help everything soften.
Cover and refrigerate at least 30 minutes, though it's best after a few hours or overnight, when the flavor turns properly tangy.

name: Salvadoran Yuca Frita
steps: Boil the cassava chunks until just tender, about 15 minutes, then drain and cool slightly.
Fry the cassava pieces in the oil until crisp and golden on the outside.
Serve topped with the shredded cabbage and a squeeze of lime if you have it.
steps_detailed: Boil the cassava chunks in salted water 15-18 minutes until just fork-tender but still holding their shape; drain and let cool enough to handle.
Cut the cassava into thick sticks, discarding the fibrous core if there is one.
Heat the vegetable oil in a skillet over medium-high heat and fry the cassava sticks in batches, 3-4 minutes per side, until deeply golden and crisp.
Drain briefly, then top with the shredded cabbage, diced tomatoes, and a squeeze of lime juice.

name: Nicaraguan Vigoron
steps: Boil the cassava until fork-tender, about 20 minutes.
Fry the pork cubes until crisp and well browned.
Plate the cassava topped with the pork, cabbage, and diced tomatoes.
steps_detailed: Boil the cassava in salted water 20-25 minutes until completely fork-tender; drain and keep warm.
Season the pork with black pepper and cook it slowly in a dry skillet over medium heat, 15-18 minutes, until browned and crisp, letting its own fat render.
Toss the cabbage, carrot, red onion, and diced tomatoes with the lime juice for a bright, crunchy slaw.
Plate the warm cassava, top with the crispy pork, and pile the slaw generously over both.

name: Nicaraguan Gallo Pinto
steps: Saute the onion in the oil until soft.
Add the black beans with a little of their liquid and the cooked rice.
Fry everything together, stirring often, until the rice takes on a light color from the beans.
steps_detailed: Cook the rice ahead of time (or use leftover rice - this dish is traditionally made with rice from the day before).
Saute the onion, bell pepper, and garlic in the vegetable oil over medium heat for 4-5 minutes until soft.
Add the black beans along with a few tablespoons of their liquid, and simmer 3-4 minutes.
Add the rice and fry everything together over medium-high heat, stirring and pressing frequently, for 6-8 minutes until the rice is evenly speckled and lightly crisp in spots.
Stir in the cilantro just before serving.

name: Nicaraguan Indio Viejo
steps: Brown the ground beef with the onion until cooked through.
Whisk the masa harina into the diced tomatoes and a cup of water until smooth, then stir into the beef.
Simmer 10-12 minutes, stirring often, until thickened into a stew.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground beef with the onion, bell pepper, and garlic over medium-high heat, 6-8 minutes, until the meat is cooked through and the vegetables are soft.
Stir in the diced tomatoes, oregano, and cumin, and simmer 5 minutes.
In a separate bowl, whisk the masa harina into the beef broth until completely smooth with no lumps.
Pour the masa mixture into the pot, stirring constantly to prevent clumping, and bring to a gentle simmer.
Cook, stirring often, for 12-15 minutes until the stew thickens to a hearty, spoonable consistency.

name: Costa Rican Casado
steps: Grill or pan-sear the chicken breast until cooked through, then let rest and slice.
Fry the plantain slices until golden on both sides.
Plate the rice, beans, plantain, cabbage, and sliced chicken together.
steps_detailed: Season the chicken breast with black pepper. Heat a splash of the vegetable oil in a skillet over medium-high heat and cook the chicken 5-6 minutes per side until cooked through and lightly charred. Rest 5 minutes, then slice.
In the same skillet, fry the plantain slices in the remaining oil, 2-3 minutes per side, until golden and caramelized at the edges.
Toss the cabbage and carrot with the lime juice for a quick slaw.
Plate the rice and black beans side by side, then arrange the sliced chicken, fried plantain, and slaw around them - the classic composed casado plate.

name: Costa Rican Gallo Pinto
steps: Saute the onion until soft.
Add the cooked rice, black beans, and Worcestershire sauce.
Fry together over medium-high heat, stirring often, until well combined and lightly toasted.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, bell pepper, and garlic in the vegetable oil over medium heat for 4-5 minutes until softened.
Add the black beans with a splash of their liquid and simmer 3-4 minutes to let the flavors meld.
Add the rice and Worcestershire sauce, and fry everything together over medium-high heat for 6-8 minutes, stirring and pressing frequently, until evenly colored and a little crisp in spots.
Stir in the cilantro just before serving.

name: Costa Rican Picadillo de Chayote
steps: Brown the ground beef with the onion until cooked through.
Add the diced chayote and tomatoes, cover, and simmer 12-15 minutes until the chayote is tender.
Season to taste and serve.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground beef with the onion, bell pepper, and garlic over medium-high heat, 6-7 minutes, until cooked through.
Stir in the diced chayote, diced tomatoes, and cumin.
Cover and simmer over medium-low heat 12-15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the chayote is tender but still holds its shape.
Uncover and simmer 3-4 more minutes to thicken slightly if there's excess liquid.
Stir in the cilantro just before serving.

name: Panamanian Sancocho
steps: Simmer the chicken thigh in water 15 minutes.
Add the cassava, plantain, and corn, and continue simmering 20 minutes until the vegetables are tender.
Season to taste and serve hot.
steps_detailed: Combine the chicken thigh, onion, bay leaf, black pepper, and chicken broth with 2 cups water in a large pot. Bring to a simmer and cook 20 minutes until the chicken is nearly cooked through.
Add the cassava and simmer 12-15 minutes until it starts to soften.
Add the plantain and corn, and continue simmering 12-15 minutes more until everything is tender and the broth has thickened slightly from the starchy vegetables.
Remove the bay leaf, stir in the cilantro, and serve hot in bowls.

name: Panamanian Patacones
steps: Fry the plantain slices 2-3 minutes per side until just golden.
Remove and flatten each piece firmly with the bottom of a glass or plate.
Fry the flattened pieces again 2 minutes per side until crisp, then drain and salt.
steps_detailed: Heat the vegetable oil in a skillet over medium heat. Fry the thick plantain slices 2-3 minutes per side until just golden but not fully crisp.
Remove and let cool slightly, then flatten each piece firmly between two pieces of parchment or with the bottom of a glass until about half its original thickness.
If using, stir the minced garlic into the frying oil for a minute to infuse it.
Return the flattened plantains to the hot oil and fry again, 2-3 minutes per side, until deeply golden and crisp at the edges.
Drain on a paper towel and salt generously while hot.
